summary:
The domestication of the common sunflower (Helianthus annuus) in eastern North America represents one of the clearest examples of an indigenous North American crop domestication, with evidence pointing to the region around present-day Tennessee, Missouri, and Arkansas as the probable center of origin. Archaeological evidence suggests that sunflower cultivation and the selection of domesticated forms with enlarged seed heads and reduced seed dispersal mechanisms began between approximately 4500 and 3000 BCE, making it one of the earliest known plant domestications north of Mexico.

Wild sunflowers are native to a broad area of North America, and their seeds, rich in oil and protein, were harvested by hunter-gatherer communities long before deliberate cultivation began. Domesticated sunflowers are distinguished from their wild relatives by significantly larger achenes (seeds) and reduced branching — traits reflecting human selection for higher seed yield and easier harvesting. Domesticated sunflower seeds are among the first crops documented in the archaeological record of [[event:1599|Archaic Eastern Woodlands]] communities, predating the arrival of the Mesoamerican crop package (maize, beans, squash) by several thousand years.

The sunflower's importance in Archaic and Woodland period Eastern Woodlands societies was significant: its seeds provided high-energy food, its oil had culinary and possibly cosmetic uses, and the plant itself was used for dye and fiber. The domestication of the sunflower demonstrates that North America north of Mexico was an independent center of agricultural innovation, where native communities developed their own crop plants and agricultural traditions centuries before contact with Mesoamerican agricultural systems.
